one_liner: A 32-year-old female with heavy menstrual periods presents with progressive fatigue, exertional dyspnea, and pallor for 3 months.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient reports progressive fatigue over the past 3 months that has worsened significantly in the past 2 weeks. She now becomes short of breath walking up one flight of stairs, which she previously did without difficulty. She has noticed increased hair loss and brittle nails. She reports heavy menstrual periods lasting 7-8 days with frequent pad changes (every 1-2 hours on her heaviest days). She also endorses pica, specifically craving and chewing ice frequently. She denies melena, hematochezia, hematemesis, or hematuria. She follows a mostly vegetarian diet with limited red meat intake. She denies any weight loss, fevers, or night sweats.
stage2.pmh: Menorrhagia (not yet formally evaluated), otherwise healthy
stage2.medications: Ibuprofen 400 mg as needed for menstrual cramps (uses 4-6 days per month)
stage2.family_history: Mother with uterine fibroids requiring hysterectomy at age 48, no history of bleeding disorders or hematologic malignancies
stage2.social_history: Non-smoker, drinks alcohol socially (1-2 glasses of wine per week). Works as a schoolteacher. Mostly vegetarian diet. No illicit drug use. Sexually active with one male partner, uses condoms for contraception.
stage2.physical_exam: General: Thin-appearing young woman, appears fatigued but in no acute distress. HEENT: Conjunctival pallor bilaterally, glossitis with smooth tongue, angular cheilitis at bilateral oral commissures. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, grade II/VI systolic flow murmur heard best at the left sternal border, no gallops. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, no hepatosplenomegaly. Extremities: No edema. Skin: Pale skin, koilonychia (spoon-shaped nails) noted on several fingernails, hair appears thin.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Moderate anemia explaining the patient's symptoms of fatigue and exertional dyspnea
Microcytic anemia; classic for this condition
Hypochromic red cells consistent with this condition
Elevated RDW (anisocytosis) is characteristic of this condition, helps distinguish from thalassemia trait
Low serum iron confirms this condition
Ferritin < 15 ng/mL is highly specific for this condition; this is the single best test
Elevated TIBC reflects the body's attempt to increase iron absorption in iron-deficient states
Inappropriately low reticulocyte response indicates impaired RBC production due to this condition
Reactive thrombocytosis is common in this condition

lab_results.result: Microcytic, hypochromic red cells with pencil cells and target cells; no schistocytes
lab_results.clinical_significance: Morphology consistent with the suspected diagnosis

imaging_results.result: Enlarged uterus measuring 10 x 6 x 5 cm with a 3 cm intramural fibroid in the posterior wall. Endometrial stripe 8 mm. Ovaries appear normal.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Uterine fibroid likely contributing to menorrhagia and subsequent this condition

low_yield_tests: Iron staining of marrow (gold standard but invasive and unnecessary here)

stage5.final_clue: The patient reports that she has been craving and chewing 2-3 cups of ice daily for the past month — a form of pica called pagophagia, which is highly specific for this condition.

debrief.classic_presentation: Iron deficiency anemia typically presents with insidious onset of fatigue, exertional dyspnea, pallor, and sometimes pica (especially pagophagia). Physical exam may reveal conjunctival pallor, glossitis, angular cheilitis, and koilonychia. In premenopausal women, menorrhagia is the most common cause. Labs show microcytic hypochromic anemia with low ferritin, low serum iron, high TIBC, and low transferrin saturation.
debrief.pathophysiology: Iron is essential for hemoglobin synthesis. When iron stores are depleted (from chronic blood loss, inadequate dietary intake, or malabsorption), the bone marrow cannot produce adequate hemoglobin, resulting in smaller (microcytic) and paler (hypochromic) red blood cells. The progression is: iron depletion (low ferritin) → iron-deficient erythropoiesis (low transferrin saturation) → iron deficiency anemia (low hemoglobin with microcytosis).
debrief.treatment_overview: Oral iron supplementation is first-line: ferrous sulfate 325 mg (65 mg elemental iron) two to three times daily on an empty stomach, ideally with vitamin C to enhance absorption. Treatment should continue for 3-6 months after hemoglobin normalizes to replete iron stores. Address the underlying cause — in this case, gynecology referral for management of menorrhagia and uterine fibroids. IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose or iron sucrose) is indicated for intolerance, malabsorption, or need for rapid repletion. Transfusion for symptomatic severe anemia or hemodynamic instability.
high_yield_pearls: Ferritin is the single best initial test for iron deficiency — a level < 15 ng/mL is virtually diagnostic
Elevated RDW helps distinguish iron deficiency anemia (high RDW) from thalassemia trait (normal RDW)
Pagophagia (ice craving) is a form of pica that is highly specific for iron deficiency
Always identify and treat the underlying cause of iron loss — in premenopausal women, menorrhagia is most common; in men and postmenopausal women, GI blood loss must be excluded
sources: UpToDate: Iron deficiency anemia in adults — Causes, clinical manifestations, and diagnosis

after_stage2_more_likely: Iron deficiency anemia (menorrhagia, pica, koilonychia, glossitis, angular cheilitis — all classic findings)
after_stage2_less_likely: Thalassemia trait (usually asymptomatic, no koilonychia or pica)
Anemia of chronic disease (no chronic illness identified)
after_labs_top3: Combined iron deficiency and anemia of chronic disease
